title: Benn Steil on Why the Global Order is Falling Apart - The Wire China
gist: CFR economist Benn Steil argues the WTO is effectively dead — killed by China's post-2001 distortions and the U.S.'s own national-security tariff abuses — and that Trump-era bilateralism, dollar weaponization, and China's turn to Carl Schmitt's illiberal theory mean the world is reverting to a pre-1995, sphere-of-influence order rather than any Bretton Woods or Marshall Plan revival.

themes.theme: The WTO's Collapse and GATT's Lesson

insights.claim: The WTO, created in 1995, was the high point of American post-Cold War universalism, building a genuinely supranational (not merely intergovernmental) dispute-settlement and appellate system whose rulings were highly consequential — a form of governance not replicated in any other policy area.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Understanding the WTO's uniquely supranational design explains why its current paralysis is a bigger institutional loss than a typical treaty failure.
insights.locator: The WTO really was the high point of American universalism

insights.claim: The 'China Shock' beginning in the late 2000s destroyed the WTO's credibility: China's economy is now almost fifteen times larger than when it joined in 2001, and by the 2010s Chinese state-capitalist distortions of trade had become severe, while rapid U.S.-China economic integration decimated electorally important U.S. manufacturing constituencies in states like Pennsylvania and Ohio.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Ties today's U.S. protectionism directly to a traceable economic-political chain: China's WTO-era growth to Rust Belt job losses to the political conditions that enabled Trump.
insights.locator: What destroyed it was the “China Shock”

insights.claim: The WTO could not discipline Chinese state subsidies because they were not explicitly tied to export performance and so did not technically violate WTO rules, even as the WTO ruled against U.S. protectionist responses.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A structural loophole, not just bad faith, let China's subsidy model escape WTO discipline — any future rules-based fix would need to close this specific gap.
insights.locator: Massive government subsidies to build up strategically important industries

insights.claim: The WTO's appellate body has been effectively dead since the U.S. blocked judge reappointments: Obama refused to reappoint one American appellate judge, and since Trump's first term no new appellate judge has been approved, leaving the body inquorate.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The WTO's dispute-settlement paralysis long predates Trump's second term and spans both parties, meaning restoration would require a bipartisan reversal that is not currently on the table.
insights.locator: Under the Obama administration, we began to see the beginnings

insights.claim: The U.S. practice of declaring protectionist measures 'national security' matters (immune from WTO adjudication) was copied globally, with countries protecting cocoa beans, door frames, and alcoholic beverages under the same rationale — effectively killing both most-favored-nation status and the dispute settlement mechanism.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The U.S. itself set the precedent that unraveled the rules-based trading system it built, a self-inflicted erosion of the regime it once championed.
insights.locator: The second thing that killed the WTO

insights.claim: The GATT (created 1947 with 23 countries) succeeded where the WTO failed because it demanded 'systemic compatibility': the Soviet Union was excluded because it would not adopt transparent, market-based trade mechanisms, whereas China was admitted to the WTO in 2001 without that requirement.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
entities: General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade
insights.so_what: The core structural fix Steil implies for any future trade order is excluding non-market economies, a direct policy signal for how a post-WTO regime might be designed around China.
insights.locator: Why was the GATT successful? The answer is pretty simple

insights.claim: Reciprocal tariff reduction as a policy tool only works when prices and production genuinely respond to market incentives, which is why applying GATT/WTO logic to a state-directed economy like China's was flawed from the start.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A generalizable rule for evaluating any future trade agreement with non-market economies: reciprocity only functions if both sides' prices respond to market signals.
insights.quote: Reciprocal tariff reduction only works when prices and production respond to market incentives.
insights.locator: Reciprocal tariff reduction only works when prices

insights.claim: Bill Clinton's argument for admitting China to the WTO — that integration would inevitably push China toward becoming a liberal democratic market economy (the Fukuyama convergence thesis) — turned out to be wrong.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The foundational assumption behind two decades of U.S. China trade policy has been falsified, which is the intellectual predicate for the current bipartisan turn toward restriction.
insights.locator: In the case of China, it's fascinating reading the comments

themes.theme: Trump-Era Trade Architecture vs. Historical Analogies

insights.claim: USTR Jamieson Greer's proposed U.S.-China Board of Trade is, in Steil's view, 'the opposite of the GATT' — designed to institutionalize state control over every aspect of bilateral trade rather than liberalize it, and unlikely to actually quash U.S.-China confrontations since both sides will keep using economic weapons to defend their interests.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Investors should not expect a new bilateral trade institution to reduce U.S.-China tension; Steil frames it as managed conflict, not managed peace.
insights.quote: It's not meant to liberalize trade. It's meant to go in the other direction entirely and institutionalize state control of every aspect of trade.
insights.locator: I would say it's the opposite of the GATT

insights.claim: Steil rejects Greer's framing of Trump's bilateral trade deals as a new 'Turnberry system' successor to Bretton Woods, arguing Bretton Woods aimed to underpin a liberalized global trading regime with stable exchange rates, whereas Trump's approach is ad hoc, rewritten week to week for political or personal reasons, and doesn't even merit being called a 'system.'
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Discounts any narrative that current U.S. trade policy represents a coherent, durable new global architecture — investors should price it as volatile and personality-driven rather than institutionalized.
insights.quote: I don't even think what the Trump administration is doing merits the term “system,” because there's nothing systematic to it. The president is constantly rewriting the rules from week to week.
insights.locator: To compare what this administration is doing to Bretton Woods

insights.claim: Steil also dismisses comparisons of current foreign 'investment commitments' into the U.S. to the Marshall Plan: the Marshall Plan was a grant-in-aid scheme requiring European cooperation, whereas today's commitments are self-interested investments by the EU, Japan, and Korea, and 'in many cases not even real' — mostly announcements that go unfulfilled or simply echo prior commitments.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Headline dollar figures for foreign 'investment pledges' tied to Trump trade deals should be heavily discounted as a policy or market signal until actual disbursement is confirmed.
insights.quote: The so-called investment commitments that American trading partners are making today as part of trade deals with the Trump administration are, first of all, in many cases not even real.
insights.locator: The so-called investment commitments that American trading partners

insights.claim: Steil highlights an accounting-identity contradiction in the administration's goals: because the current account and capital account are mirror images, increasing foreign investment inflows into the U.S. (a capital account surplus) mechanically implies lower net exports — directly undercutting the stated goal of shrinking the U.S. current account deficit.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A concrete macro identity investors can use to flag internal inconsistency whenever the administration simultaneously touts new foreign investment and a shrinking trade deficit as joint wins.
insights.locator: In terms of the administration's goal to reduce the U.S. current account deficit

themes.theme: Tariffs: Legitimate Tool vs. Blunt Instrument

insights.claim: Steil says some China trade controls are defensible on national-security grounds — particularly reducing dependence on Chinese rare earths and certain technologies — but this needs to be balanced against the roughly half of U.S. imports from China that are intermediate goods American manufacturers need to remain globally competitive.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Frames tariff policy as needing sector-by-sector discrimination rather than blanket application — a useful lens for assessing which tariff categories are likely to persist versus be walked back.
insights.locator: Some of it is clearly defensible

insights.claim: Steil argues the U.S. should exploit rather than block certain forms of Chinese state capitalism: subsidized, underpriced Chinese steel benefits American steel-consuming industries, which employ roughly fifty times as many U.S. workers as steel-producing industries do.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A concrete jobs-weighted argument against blanket steel tariffs — investors in steel-consuming manufacturers should weigh this employment asymmetry when assessing tariff-policy risk.
insights.quote: American steel-using industries employ about fifty times as many American workers as American steel-producing industries.
insights.locator: About half of what we import from China are intermediate goods

insights.claim: Tariffs are a blunt instrument that struggle to distinguish genuine security risks from inputs U.S. manufacturers need to compete, and their costs fall overwhelmingly on American consumers and businesses (via the importer who pays the tariff and passes costs through), not on Chinese exporters.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Reinforces that tariff-driven inflation risk sits with U.S. consumers/importers, an important input for assessing the domestic economic cost of an escalating tariff regime.
insights.locator: Tariffs are a blunt instrument

themes.theme: China's Growth Model and Five-Year Plan Priorities

insights.claim: Steil says no credible economist, Chinese or Western, disputes that China needs significant reforms to boost domestic demand — the issue isn't that Xi Jinping's government doesn't understand this, but that boosting domestic demand simply isn't their priority relative to dominating global industrial sectors.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Investors betting on a near-term Chinese consumption-led rebalancing should note Steil's view that this is a deliberate political choice, not a knowledge gap — implying it won't change absent a political shift.
insights.locator: I don't know any credible economist

insights.claim: Steil frames China's industrial dominance strategy as simultaneously mercantilist and a form of warfare: dominating key global industries is intended to enable regional and eventually broader military and geopolitical dominance, following a version of the American industrial-policy playbook of the late 19th century.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Reframes China's industrial subsidies as a security investment, not just an economic one — investors should expect Beijing to keep subsidizing strategic sectors even at the cost of efficiency or profitability.
insights.locator: At one level, this is just pure mercantilism, but at another level, it's warfare

themes.theme: Dollar Dominance, Sanctions, and the RMB/CIPS Workaround

insights.claim: Steil compares U.S. sanctions to an over-prescribed antibiotic: heavy reliance on sanctions is encouraging the rest of the world to build resistance — i.e., alternative financial infrastructure — to immunize itself from American sanctions regimes.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A durable mental model: the more aggressively the U.S. uses sanctions, the faster it accelerates the very de-dollarization workarounds it fears.
insights.locator: The reason we love sanctions is that we can cause pain

insights.claim: There is a real but still small shift of international payments from the dollar/SWIFT to the RMB and China's CIPS system — the RMB doesn't even outrank the Canadian dollar in SWIFT transaction volume, and China's CIPS 'plumbing' is opaque to Western observers.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic scale for the 'de-dollarization' narrative: real and growing, but from a small base, and lacking Western visibility — a genuine but not yet material risk to dollar dominance.
insights.locator: What we're seeing in international payments is twofold

insights.claim: Steil cites the Iran conflict as a natural experiment showing RMB/CIPS use is driven by sanctions evasion, not secular de-dollarization: after the U.S. attacked Iran in late February 2026, CIPS RMB transaction volumes spiked sharply in March as Iran demanded RMB payment for strait passage; volumes and a major Chinese non-U.S.-clearing financial intermediary's stock price both dropped after the U.S. ceasefire announcement as markets bet on a return to dollar transactions.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated event study investors can use to interpret future RMB/CIPS volume spikes as tactical sanctions-evasion responses rather than evidence of structural de-dollarization.
insights.locator: I argued in a recent blog post that this is being driven

insights.claim: China's strategy for promoting RMB use isn't to set global monetary policy but to peg the RMB tightly to the dollar (with small politically timed moves, such as appreciating ahead of Trump's state visit to Beijing) to market it as a stable dollar alternative — functionally similar to how a dollar-pegged stablecoin operates in crypto.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A distinctive analytic frame — RMB internationalization as a 'stablecoin strategy' — for understanding why China keeps its currency pegged rather than floating it despite internationalization ambitions.
insights.quote: It's not so much that [the Chinese] want to set monetary policy for the world… But they're trying to convince the market that the RMB is, in fact, a stable alternative to the dollar, in the way that a dollar stablecoin might be in the cryptocurrency environment.
insights.locator: It's not so much that [the Chinese] want to set monetary policy

themes.theme: Europe's Structural Dependence on U.S. Security

insights.claim: Steil argues globalization and European integration depended on U.S. security guarantees, not the reverse — the liberal trading order didn't produce peace, American security guarantees produced the conditions under which liberalized trade could flourish — and that framework is now coming apart as the U.S. steps back from underwriting European security.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A causal reframing investors should apply to any 'end of globalization' narrative: it's a security-guarantee story first, a trade-policy story second.
insights.quote: That's the thing to remember: globalization depended on U.S. security guarantees, not the other way around.
insights.locator: That's the thing to remember

insights.claim: The EU's 27 members have long been unable to agree on fiscal or defense policy because the U.S. handled 'the hard stuff'; now, to secure trade deals with the U.S. and avoid heavy tariffs, Europe has been forced to move away from its own commitment to most-favored-nation treatment, sparking internal EU debate over formally proposing to replace MFN within the WTO.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Europe's retreat from MFN is a live, internally contested policy fight worth monitoring as a leading indicator of the WTO's further erosion.
insights.locator: Europe has tried to defend the WTO and multilateralism

themes.theme: Carl Schmitt and China's Alternative World-Order Vision

insights.claim: Contrary to the mainstream U.S. foreign-policy view (associated with theorists like John Ikenberry) that China is merely a 'spoiler' with no coherent alternative to the liberal rules-based order, Steil found that citations of Carl Schmitt — the 'Crown Jurist of the Third Reich' — surged in Chinese scholarly databases after 2003, indicating China does have a coherent intellectual framework for an alternative order.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers assuming China lacks a strategic doctrine for global order are working from a stale premise; Schmitt's framework offers a more accurate predictive lens for Chinese behavior.
insights.locator: I decided about a year ago to start digging into this

insights.claim: Chinese scholars such as Liu Xiaofeng have built careers using Carl Schmitt's thinking to justify the Chinese state's total power and to articulate an explicit alternative to Western liberalism.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Names a specific intellectual lineage (Liu Xiaofeng et al.) that investors and analysts can track for early signals of how Beijing's elite justifies its governance and foreign-policy model.
insights.locator: You have Chinese scholars like Liu Xiaofeng

insights.claim: Schmitt's concept of 'Großraum' — regional orders organized around a dominant power, each with its own political and legal character and no universal rule-setter — maps closely onto China's stated vision of a multipolar world with spheres of influence, and the U.S. revival of the Monroe Doctrine only reinforces Beijing's belief it is on the correct historical path toward Asia-Pacific dominance.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete conceptual model (Großraum/spheres of influence) for anticipating Chinese regional assertiveness in Asia-Pacific as ideologically, not just opportunistically, motivated.
insights.locator: Schmitt's concept of Großraum

insights.claim: Steil's overarching prediction is that Carl Schmitt, not Francis Fukuyama, is the better guide to the current geopolitical trajectory: as the U.S. abandons the WTO, weaponizes the dollar, and treats alliances transactionally, it is vindicating Schmitt's view that liberal universalism was always a mask for great-power hegemony.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The single highest-level thesis of the piece: the operating framework for interpreting U.S. and Chinese behavior going forward should be power-and-spheres-of-influence (Schmitt), not convergence-to-liberalism (Fukuyama).
insights.quote: We are becoming proof of his sense that liberal universalism was always a mask for great-power hegemony.
insights.locator: The United States is abandoning its universalist commitments

top_entities: General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade
